g.data-narration: Welcome to this lecture on Copy Constructors in C++. This is a fundamental concept for proper object initialization.
A copy constructor is a special constructor that creates a new object as an exact copy of an existing object of the same class.
g.text: A special constructor that initializes a new object using an existing object of the same class.
Takes a const reference parameter of the same class type.

g.data-narration: Copy constructors are essential for proper memory management, preventing bugs like shallow copy issues and resource leaks.
g.text: • Essential for passing/returning objects by value to/from functions
• Prevents shallow copy bugs in classes with dynamic memory
• Foundation for Rule of Three/Five and move semantics in modern C++

g.data-narration: When no copy constructor is defined, the compiler provides a default one that performs memberwise shallow copy.
g.text: If not explicitly defined, compiler provides a default copy constructor that performs memberwise copy.
For pointer members, only the address is copied - both objects share the same memory!
⚠ This leads to double-deletion bugs and undefined behavior

g.data-narration: Deep copy allocates new memory and copies actual data, while shallow copy only copies pointer addresses.
g.text: Pointer values copied • Both objects share same memory
New memory allocated • Each object has independent data

g.data-narration: Copy constructor is invoked in four main scenarios: object initialization, pass by value, return by value, and array initialization.
Copy constructor creates a NEW object during initialization, while assignment operator modifies an EXISTING object.
g.text: MyClass obj2 = obj1; // Creates NEW object

g.data-narration: Always use const reference parameter to allow copying from both const and non-const objects without modification.
g.text: Parameter MUST be a reference to avoid infinite recursion.
Use const to allow copying from both const and non-const objects.

g.data-narration: Here's a complete example showing user-defined copy constructor performing deep copy for dynamic memory.
The Rule of Three states if you define any of destructor, copy constructor, or assignment operator, define all three.
g.text: If you define any ONE of these for a class with resources, you should define ALL THREE:

g.data-narration: In derived classes, the copy constructor must explicitly call the base class copy constructor to properly initialize base members.
g.text: Derived class must explicitly call base class copy constructor:
Base(obj) calls Base copy constructor to copy base members

g.data-narration: Prevent object copying by making copy constructor private or using C++11 delete specifier.
Let's summarize the key points: always use deep copy for classes with pointers, understand when copy constructor is called, and apply Rule of Three.
For exams, remember: copy constructor syntax, shallow vs deep copy differences, when it is called, and Rule of Three.
g.text: 1. Remember the signature: ClassName(const ClassName and obj)
2. Default performs memberwise shallow copy - dangerous for pointer members
3. Copy Constructor creates NEW object | Assignment modifies EXISTING
4. Called: Init, Pass-by-value, Return-by-value, Array init
5. Rule of Three: Destructor + Copy Ctor + Assignment
6. Use = delete to prevent copying in C++11
